The natives believe that if this is pointed at any member of a tribe, nothing on earth can save the victim from death. They are so convinced of this, that immediately it is done his spirits droop, he becomes melancholy, his appetite fails, and gradually he pines away and dies. Should the Koonkie fail in his effort to relieve the sick, he is prepared with a ready excuse — some Eoonkie of another tribe, possessing more skill, has stopped his power. When a Koonkie is ill he calls in the aid of another Koonkie to cure him. As I have said elsewhere, no person is presumed to become ill naturally. The Kootchie (devil), or some native, has bewitched him. CURE OF DISEASE OR WOUNDS. Sores, cuts, bruises, pain, and diseases of all kinds, no matter how arising, are treated in one of two modes; if slight, by the application of dirt to the part affected; if severe, by that of hot ashes. Their notions of medicine, as amongst all savages, were of the mast absurd nature. Amongst the Adelaide tribe the sorcerers were the physicians. All internal pains were supposed to arise from witchcraft, and were variously treated. Sometimes the blood was sucked out from the part affected, and sometimes a bone was supposed to be extracted fi^m it by suction. On other occasions the disease was taken away in an invisible form, and either burnt or thrown into water. In.

evidence_summary: . Another object of terror to the aborigines is called "koonyoo," who flies about at night, and makes a noise in the trees, but is never seen. This being descends to the earth in the dark, alights upon the body of a man while sleeping, and presses on his liver, causing him to sufler excessive pains, and sometimes producing death. This is nothing more than a supernatural cause to which is attributed the eflect of excessive gorging, which the natives indulge in whenever they have the opportunity. It is remarkable that none of the tribes appear to be under the authority of a chief. A certain amount of influence, however, is exercised.
